title: The E-Myth Revisited
subtitle: Why Most Small Businesses Don't Work and What to Do About It
themes: The entrepreneurial myth — technical skill is not business acumen
Working ON the business, not IN it
The three personalities inside every owner
Business as a replicable franchise prototype
Structure and process as paths to freedom
excerpt: Most small businesses fail not because the owner lacks technical skill, but because they suffer from the Entrepreneurial Myth — the fatal assumption that if you understand the technical work of a business, you understand a business that does that technical work. Gerber's solution: stop working IN your business and start working ON it. Build systems so clear and replicable that your business could be franchised 5,000 times, even if you never sell a single franchise.
whoShouldRead: Technicians — bakers, coders, mechanics, consultants — thinking of starting their own business
Small business owners who feel like they are the business (it stops when they stop)
Entrepreneurs struggling to scale beyond a one-person operation
Anyone who confuses being good at a craft with being good at running a business
whoShouldSkip: Experienced franchise operators already running multi-unit businesses
Readers looking for specific marketing tactics or financial how-to
Those who prefer dense data over extended analogy and storytelling
keyIdeas: The E-Myth — most businesses are started by technicians having an entrepreneurial seizure, not by true entrepreneurs
Three personalities — every owner has an Entrepreneur (vision), Manager (order), and Technician (doing); letting the Technician dominate is fatal
Business life cycle — Infancy (owner = business), Adolescence (hiring help, growing pains), Maturity (systems run the business)
Franchise Prototype — build your business as if it will be replicated 5,000 times; the real product is the business itself
Working ON vs IN — the owner's job is to design the system, not be the system
Business Development Process — a continuous cycle of Innovation, Quantification, and Orchestration
Seven-step Business Development Program — Primary Aim, Strategic Objective, Organizational Strategy, Management Strategy, People Strategy, Marketing Strategy, Systems Strategy
Position contracts — define every role by results, not by person; separate the person from the position
The franchise mindset — systems-dependent, not people-dependent; ordinary people can produce extraordinary results with the right system
relatedBooks: Company of One by Paul Jarvis
The Lean Startup by Eric Ries
Built to Last by Jim Collins
The Great CEO Within by Matt Mochary
